narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30 and plans to attend law school full-time, you've put real effort into your application, and I appreciate how challenging this process can feel. The admissions model we use predicted a 51% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection—it's designed to flag higher-risk profiles to help the school maintain strong bar passage rates. This decision hinged on a few key aspects of your academic record that stood out as the strongest influences. Most notably, your position in the second decile for Year 3 grades at your school pushed the prediction toward failure more than any other factor, signaling to the model that your performance there lagged behind what it typically sees in successful bar takers. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA actually worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward passing since it's above the average of 3.21 across past students. Your lower-middle family income bracket also helped your case, as the model associates it with resilience that aids bar success compared to typical applicants. Meanwhile, your second-decile ranking in Year 1 grades and your LSAT score of 28—below the usual 36.7 average—nudged things slightly toward the riskier side, though less powerfully than your Year 3 performance. Compared to the broader pool of students, where most pursue full-time programs like you, your profile shows strengths in GPA and background but academics that fall short of the top performers who usually pass the bar. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male applicant—many in similar demographics succeed with targeted improvements—it's just the model's data-driven read. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the average, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or tutoring, and highlighting any personal growth stories in a reapplication. You're close, and with these steps, you could shift that prediction in your favor next time—keep pushing, you've got this.
